planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in full sun in well-drained, fertile to average soil; Snake Bean Tree is a West and Central African legume tree producing extraordinary, long, twisted, snake-like seed pods hanging in pendulous clusters from the branches — pods reach 1.5 to 3 feet long — and bears white and yellow pea-family flowers in striking axillary clusters.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to poor soil; Swartzia madagascariensis is drought tolerant once established and adapted to seasonal dry conditions — water regularly in the first two years for establishment, then reduce to deep, infrequent watering or rely on seasonal rainfall in tropical climates. Swartzia madagascariensis (Snake Bean Tree or Worm Tree) is a large, semi-deciduous to evergreen legume tree native to the woodland savannas, miombo forests, and bushveld of tropical and sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal east through Ethiopia and south through East Africa to Zimbabwe and Mozambique, where it is recognized as one of the most distinctive fruiting trees of the African savanna by its extraordinarily long, pendulous, corded, snake-like or worm-like seed pods — each 18 to 36 inches long, twisted and constricted between seeds, hanging in spectacular cascades from the branches. The creamy-white and yellow flowers carried in dense clusters attract pollinators before the pods develop. The hard, heavy, dark-reddish-brown heartwood is highly valued as a traditional timber for construction, tool handles, musical instruments, and carving across the tree's range. The bark and roots are widely used in traditional African medicine for pain relief, wound healing, and anti-parasitic applications. An exceptional specimen tree for large tropical gardens, wildlife areas, and agroforestry systems.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Swartzia madagascariensis

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in full sun in well-drained, fertile, deep soil; African Rosewood is a large, deciduous West African savanna tree of great commercial and ecological importance — its heartwood is one of the most internationally traded and threatened tropical timbers, prized for furniture and instruments — and it bears brilliant yellow pea-family flowers in terminal clusters before leaf-out in the dry season.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile soil; Pterocarpus erinaceus is a drought-adapted savanna tree that requires excellent drainage and tolerates seasonal dry periods — avoid waterlogged sites and heavy clay soils. Pterocarpus erinaceus (African Rosewood, Vène, or Kosso) is a large, deciduous legume tree native to the open savanna, guinea woodland, and dry forest zones of West Africa from Senegal east through the Sudan-Sahel zone to Ethiopia, a member of the Fabaceae family and one of the continent's most economically significant and critically over-exploited timber trees. The heartwood is dense, durable, deep reddish-brown to rose-red with interlocked grain — superficially resembling Asian rosewoods (Dalbergia spp.) — and has been the subject of massive illegal logging and export particularly to China and East Asia in the 21st century for furniture manufacture, resulting in international CITES trade regulation. Before logging, trees produce spectacular, dense, terminal clusters of deep-yellow pea-family flowers in the dry season on leafless branches. The tree is a critical component of the West African parkland agroforestry system where it is retained in farmers' fields for shade, nitrogen fixation, fodder, and traditional medicine (the red-staining bark gum is used medicinally across West Africa). A valuable shade and nitrogen-fixing tree for tropical agroforestry systems where large specimens can be grown sustainably.
sources: FAO Forestry Paper — Pterocarpus in West African Parklands

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade to partial shade in zone 8–10; Black Cardamom is a large, clumping, ginger-family perennial native to the cool, moist, shaded mountain forests of the Sikkim Himalayas, Nepal, and Bhutan, where the large, dark-brown, wrinkled, smoke-dried seed pods are harvested as a distinctive warming spice with a camphor-menthol-smoke flavor characteristic of North Indian cuisine.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained but consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in deep to partial shade; Black Cardamom thrives in cool, moist conditions resembling its native Himalayan forest understory — mulch heavily, maintain consistent moisture, and protect from hot afternoon sun and frost. Amomum subulatum (Black Cardamom, Large Cardamom, or Nepal Cardamom) is a large, clumping, ginger-family rhizomatous perennial native to the cool, misty, shaded montane forests of Sikkim (India), Nepal, Bhutan, and adjacent mountain regions, where it grows wild at elevations of 3,000–5,000 feet in dense forest understory along streams and in humid ravines. The plant is cultivated extensively under Alnus nepalensis alder trees in the traditional 'Sikkim Cardamom' agroforestry system, which provides the required dense shade, consistent moisture, and forest microclimate. The seed pods — harvested and kiln-dried or traditionally fire-dried over bamboo smoke — are large (1–1.5 inch), dark brown, wrinkled, and distinctively smoky-camphor-menthol in aroma and flavor, completely different from green cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum). Black cardamom is a fundamental spice in North Indian biryani, dal makhani, garam masala blends, and many Nepali and Tibetan dishes; it is also used medicinally in Ayurvedic, Tibetan, and Chinese traditional medicine. A superb shade-garden spice plant for warm, humid zone 8–10 climates; the large, banana-like foliage is ornamentally attractive even when not in pod.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Black Cardamom Cultivation

subcategory: South Asian timber and shade tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, deep, fertile to average soil in zone 8–12; Indian Rosewood (Sheesham) is a large, deciduous to semi-evergreen South Asian legume tree and one of the premier timber trees of the Indian subcontinent — fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing, and drought-tolerant — bearing small, pale-pink, fragrant pea-family flowers and widely planted across South Asia and the Middle East as a street tree and windbreak.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile to average soil; Dalbergia sissoo is drought tolerant once established, fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root symbiosis, and can improve soil fertility on degraded or depleted sites — water regularly only in the first year of establishment. Dalbergia sissoo (Indian Rosewood, Sheesham, or Sissoo) is a large, fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing deciduous legume tree native to the Indo-Gangetic Plain foothills, riverine forests, and lower Himalayan slopes of India, Nepal, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan, where it is one of the most important and commercially valuable timber trees of South Asia. The dense, interlocked, dark-brown to reddish-brown heartwood is among the finest furniture, carving, flooring, and musical instrument timbers of the region, with extraordinary durability and beautiful figuring. A nitrogen-fixer in the legume family (Fabaceae), Dalbergia sissoo enriches impoverished soils through atmospheric nitrogen fixation and organic matter return. Small, fragrant, pale-pink to whitish pea-family flowers attract pollinators. Trees are widely planted across India, Pakistan, and the Middle East as street trees, windbreaks, and shade trees for their fast growth, nitrogen-fixing soil improvement, drought tolerance, and ornamental foliage with rounded, tri-foliate, bright-green leaflets. It has naturalized in parts of Florida and the Caribbean and can become invasive — plant with awareness of local invasive status.
sources: USDA Forest Service — Dalbergia sissoo profile

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 8–11; White Cross Berry is a drought-tolerant African savanna shrub or small tree with distinctive bicolor (green above, white/silver beneath) leaves bearing small, yellow star-shaped flowers and round, reddish-orange to dark-brown edible berries eaten across sub-Saharan Africa as a bush food and used medicinally.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, average to poor soil; Grewia bicolor is highly drought tolerant and adapted to seasonal dry and semi-arid conditions — once established it requires little or no supplemental water in warm arid and semi-arid climates. Grewia bicolor (White Cross Berry or Crossberry) is a thorny, much-branched shrub or small tree native to the dry, open savannas, bushveld, and semi-arid woodland of sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal east through Ethiopia and south through East Africa to South Africa and Mozambique — one of the most widely distributed and ecologically important shrubs of African savanna ecosystems. The distinctive bicolor leaves — deep green and glossy above, densely silver-white hairy and felted beneath — give the plant a shimmering two-tone appearance in the breeze. Small, 5-petaled, yellow or cream star-shaped flowers are borne prolifically from the leaf axils in summer, followed by round to oblong, 2–4-lobed berries — ripening from green through reddish-orange to dark brown or black — that are sweet-mucilaginous, edible raw or dried, and important bush food across the plant's range. The berries are browsed by elephants, giraffes, and many antelope species, and are eaten by indigenous peoples dried as a sweetener or fermented into beer. The roots and bark carry extensive traditional medicinal uses across sub-Saharan Africa for fevers, diarrhea, and sexually transmitted infections. An excellent drought-tolerant, wildlife-attracting shrub for hot, dry, sunny gardens in zone 8–11.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; Burmese Grape (Latka, Mafai) is a medium-sized tropical tree native to Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, and Southeast Asia that produces its clustered grape-like fruits directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflory) — round, yellow to pinkish-red, thin-skinned fruits with juicy, sweet-sour white pulp surrounding large seeds.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Baccaurea ramiflora benefits from consistent moisture, high humidity, and protection from strong drying winds — mulch heavily and water regularly throughout the growing season. Baccaurea ramiflora (Burmese Grape, Latka, or Mafai) is an evergreen to semi-deciduous tree of the Phyllanthaceae family native to Bangladesh, Myanmar, Bhutan, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, and south into the Malay Peninsula, where it is cultivated and semi-wild in forest edges, village gardens, and homestead agroforestry systems. The tree exhibits cauliflory — fruiting directly on the trunk and large branches in dense, pendulous racemes — producing small (1–2 inch), round to slightly oval, yellow-to-pinkish-red, thin-skinned fruits with translucent, white, sweet-sour, mucilaginous pulp around large seeds. The fruits are eaten fresh when ripe, made into jams, pickles, and fermented drinks, and are an important local market fruit in Bangladesh (where it is called Lotkon), Thailand (Mafai thet), and Myanmar. The tree is dioecious — male and female trees are separate — so plant at least one male with every 4–6 female trees. Baccaurea ramiflora is related to rambai (B. motleyana) and shares the genus's characteristic trunk-borne fruiting and acid-sweet flavor profile popular across Southeast Asian tropical fruit markets.
sources: Bangladesh Agricultural Research Institute — Baccaurea ramiflora
